title: Count occurrences of x
description: Given an array count how many times a number x appears

examples.explanation: Initialize count = 0. Check arr[0] = 2: equals x=2, count becomes 1. Check arr[1] = 3: not equal to 2, count remains 1. Check arr[2] = 2: equals 2, count becomes 2. Check arr[3] = 4: not equal to 2, count remains 2. Check arr[4] = 2: equals 2, count becomes 3. Check arr[5] = 5: not equal to 2, count remains 3. Return count = 3.

Initialize count = 0. Check arr[0] = 1: not equal to x=6, count remains 0. Check arr[1] = 2: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Check arr[2] = 3: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Check arr[3] = 4: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Check arr[5] = 5: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Return count = 0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each element in the array and increment a counter whenever the element equals x. This is straightforward and works for any unsorted array.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Occurrences of x in Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting how many times a number x appears in an array using brute force iteration.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves this problem by examining every element in the array exactly once. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to count all occurrences of x, we must check each element to see if it matches x. There's no way to avoid checking elements in an unsorted array, making this approach optimal for the general case. The algorithm initializes a counter to 0, then iterates through each element in the array. For each element, it compares the element to x. If they're equal, it increments the counter. After processing all elements, it returns the counter. This works because we systematically check every possible position where x could appear. The time complexity is O(n) where n is the array length, as we perform one comparison per element. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a constant amount of extra memory for the counter. This connects directly to the key subroutine (L1) which is the iteration and comparison logic. Common mistakes include: forgetting to initialize the counter (leading to undefined behavior), using the wrong comparison operator (especially in languages with reference vs value equality), or returning early before checking all elements. The brute force approach is fundamental because it works for any input without assumptions about sorting or structure, making it a reliable baseline solution.

examples.trace: Initialize count = 0. Check arr[0] = 2: equals x=2, count becomes 1. Check arr[1] = 3: not equal to 2, count remains 1. Check arr[2] = 2: equals 2, count becomes 2. Check arr[3] = 4: not equal to 2, count remains 2. Check arr[4] = 2: equals 2, count becomes 3. Check arr[5] = 5: not equal to 2, count remains 3. Return count = 3.

Initialize count = 0. Check arr[0] = 1: not equal to x=6, count remains 0. Check arr[1] = 2: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Check arr[2] = 3: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Check arr[3] = 4: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Check arr[5] = 5: not equal to 6, count remains 0. Return count = 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterate and Compare Elements
ladders.desc: The core loop that traverses the array and compares each element to target x.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the essential iteration and comparison logic needed for counting occurrences. The intuition is that to determine if an element should be counted, we need to examine it and check if it matches our target value. This is done through a loop that systematically accesses each array element one by one. The algorithm uses an index variable (typically i) that starts at 0 and goes up to n-1 (where n is array length). For each index position, it retrieves the element arr[i] and compares it to x using equality comparison. This comparison is the decision point: if equal, we would increment a counter (though the counter management is handled at a higher level). The time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) as it performs n iterations. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. This feeds into the full problem (L0) by providing the mechanism to examine every element - without this iteration, we couldn't count occurrences in an unsorted array. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., using i <= n instead of i < n), modifying the array during iteration (which can cause skipped elements), or using the wrong index variable. The iteration pattern is fundamental to array processing and appears in countless algorithms.

examples.trace: i=0: arr[0]=4, compare to x=4 → true. i=1: arr[1]=5, compare to 4 → false. i=2: arr[2]=4, compare to 4 → true. i=3: arr[3]=6, compare to 4 → false.
i=0: arr[0]=1, compare to x=5 → false. i=1: arr[1]=2, compare to 5 → false. i=2: arr[2]=3, compare to 5 → false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Equality Comparison Decision
ladders.desc: Determining whether two values are equal, which drives the counting condition.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the equality comparison operation that decides whether to count an element. The intuition is simple: we need a binary decision - is this element equal to our target x or not? This comparison is fundamental because it transforms each array element into a yes/no decision about whether it contributes to our count. The algorithm is just a single comparison: element == x. This returns true if they're equal, false otherwise. In the context of counting, when true, we increment our counter; when false, we don't. The time complexity is O(1) per comparison. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the iteration sub-routine (L1) by providing the condition checked at each iteration. Without this comparison, the iteration would just traverse elements without purpose. Common mistakes include: using assignment (=) instead of equality (==), floating-point comparison issues (where 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3 due to precision), or comparing objects by reference instead of value in some languages. Understanding equality comparison is crucial because it's the predicate that makes the algorithm meaningful - it's what distinguishes counting occurrences of x from just counting all elements.

examples.trace: Compare 5 == 5. Both values are equal, so result is true.
Compare 3 == 7. Values are different (3 ≠ 7), so result is false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Element Access
ladders.desc: Retrieving an element from an array using its index position.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on accessing individual elements within an array using index notation. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can retrieve any element if we know its position (index). The algorithm is straightforward: given an array arr and an index i, we access the element as arr[i]. This uses zero-based indexing where the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, etc. The time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1) as no additional memory is needed. This feeds into the comparison logic (L2) by providing the element value to compare against x. Without being able to access array elements, we couldn't examine their values. Common mistakes include: accessing out-of-bounds indices (causing errors), confusing zero-based with one-based indexing, or misunderstanding that arr[i] retrieves a value (doesn't modify the array). Array access is fundamental to working with collections of data - it's how we 'read' from arrays. In many languages, arr[i] is actually syntactic sugar for pointer arithmetic that calculates the memory address offset from the array's starting address.

examples.trace: Array arr has elements at indices: 0→10, 1→20, 2→30. Access arr[1] retrieves the element at index 1, which is 20.
Array arr has elements: index 0→5, 1→6, 2→7, 3→8. Access arr[0] retrieves the first element, which is 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Variable Assignment and Integer Counter
ladders.desc: Creating and updating a counter variable to track occurrences.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using a variable to store and update a count. The intuition is that we need a 'memory' to accumulate results as we process elements - we can't just look at each element in isolation. The algorithm involves: (1) Initializing a counter variable (e.g., count = 0), (2) Incrementing it when we find a match (e.g., count = count + 1 or count += 1). The counter starts at 0 because initially we've found no occurrences. Each time we find x in the array, we add 1 to the counter. After processing all elements, the counter holds the total occurrences. Time complexity is O(1) per increment. Space complexity is O(1) for the variable. This feeds into array element access (L3) because once we access an element and compare it, we need to record the result somehow. The counter is that recording mechanism. Common mistakes include: forgetting to initialize the counter (leading to undefined values), incrementing incorrectly (like using count++ when count hasn't been defined), or resetting the counter accidentally within a loop. This operation is fundamental to accumulation patterns in programming - many algorithms need to count, sum, or accumulate results. The counter variable represents the algorithm's 'state' that changes as we process input.

examples.trace: Start: count = 0. First increment: count = 0 + 1 = 1. Second increment: count = 1 + 1 = 2. Third increment: count = 2 + 1 = 3. Final count = 3.
Start: count = 2. Increment: count = 2 + 1 = 3. Final count = 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays as Ordered Collections
ladders.desc: Fundamental concept of arrays: ordered sequences of elements accessible by position.
ladders.explanation: This foundation level introduces the array data structure at its most basic level. An array is an ordered collection of elements where each element has a position (called an index). The intuition is that arrays let us store multiple values together in a structured way, like a row of boxes numbered 0, 1, 2, etc. Each 'box' holds one value, and we can look inside any box if we know its number. Arrays have a fixed length (number of elements) that's typically set when created. The key properties are: (1) Ordered - elements have positions, (2) Indexable - we can access elements by their position, (3) Homogeneous - usually all elements are the same type, (4) Contiguous storage - elements are stored next to each other in memory. This concept feeds into variable assignment (L4) because arrays themselves are variables that hold collections rather than single values. Common beginner mistakes include: thinking arrays start at index 1 (they usually start at 0), confusing arrays with other collections like sets, or not understanding that array length is fixed. Arrays are fundamental because they're the simplest way to store and process multiple related values - almost every algorithm uses them. In our counting problem, the array is the input we need to examine.

examples.trace: This array has 3 elements. The first element (position 0) is 5. The second element (position 1) is 10. The third element (position 2) is 15. The length is 3.
An empty array has length 0. There are no positions/elements. It's like having zero boxes.

approaches.name: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: If the array is sorted, use binary search to find the first and last occurrence of x, then compute the count as the difference plus one. This leverages sorted order for efficiency.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Occurrences Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution: find first and last occurrence of x in sorted array using binary search, then compute count.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages the sorted nature of the array to achieve O(log n) time complexity. The intuition is that in a sorted array, all occurrences of x will be contiguous. By finding the leftmost (first) and rightmost (last) indices where x appears, we can compute the count as (last - first + 1). We use two separate binary search functions: one to find the first occurrence by continuing to search left when x is found, and another to find the last occurrence by continuing to search right when x is found. The algorithm: 1) If array is empty, return 0. 2) Find first occurrence index using binary search. 3) If x not found (first == -1), return 0. 4) Find last occurrence index using binary search. 5) Return (last - first + 1). Time: O(log n) for two binary searches. Space: O(1). This connects to L1 by using the helper functions for first/last occurrence. Common mistakes: not handling empty arrays, off-by-one errors in binary search conditions, not checking if x exists before computing count. Real code pattern: def count_occurrences(arr, x): if not arr: return 0; first = find_first(arr, x); if first == -1: return 0; last = find_last(arr, x); return last - first + 1.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find first occurrence of 2. Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2 (arr[2]=2, found). Since found, move left: high=1. low=0, high=1, mid=0 (arr[0]=1 < 2), low=1. low=1, high=1, mid=1 (arr[1]=2, found). Update first=1, move left: high=0. low=1 > high=0, exit. First occurrence at index 1. Step 2: Find last occurrence of 2. Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2 (arr[2]=2, found). Since found, move right: low=3. low=3, high=5, mid=4 (arr[4]=3 > 2), high=3. low=3, high=3, mid=3 (arr[3]=2, found). Update last=3, move right: low=4. low=4 > high=3, exit. Last occurrence at index 3. Step 3: Count = 3 - 1 + 1 = 3.

Step 1: Find first occurrence of 6. Binary search: low=0, high=5, mid=2 (arr[2]=7 > 6), high=1. low=0, high=1, mid=0 (arr[0]=5 < 6), low=1. low=1, high=1, mid=1 (arr[1]=7 > 6), high=0. low=1 > high=0, exit. No first occurrence found (first = -1). Step 2: Since first == -1, return 0 directly without finding last occurrence.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find First Occurrence Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Binary search modified to find the leftmost index where x appears in sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This helper function is crucial because it efficiently locates the starting point of x's occurrences. The intuition: in standard binary search, when we find x at mid, we return immediately. Here, we continue searching left (by setting high = mid - 1) to see if there's an earlier occurrence. We track the result in a variable 'result' initialized to -1. Algorithm: 1) Initialize low=0, high=n-1, result=-1. 2) While low <= high: a) mid = low + (high - low)//2. b) If arr[mid] == x: set result = mid, then search left: high = mid - 1. c) Else if arr[mid] < x: search right: low = mid + 1. d) Else: search left: high = mid - 1. 3) Return result. Time: O(log n). Space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the first index needed to compute count. Common mistakes: forgetting to update result when x is found, infinite loop if not adjusting bounds correctly, off-by-one in mid calculation. Real code pattern: def find_first(arr, x): low, high, result = 0, len(arr)-1, -1; while low <= high: mid = low + (high - low)//2; if arr[mid] == x: result = mid; high = mid - 1; elif arr[mid] < x: low = mid + 1; else: high = mid - 1; return result.

examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=2 (arr[2]=2 == x). Set result=2, search left: high=1. Step 2: low=0, high=1, mid=0 (arr[0]=1 < x), search right: low=1. Step 3: low=1, high=1, mid=1 (arr[1]=2 == x). Set result=1, search left: high=0. Step 4: low=1 > high=0, exit loop. Return result=1.

Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=2 (arr[2]=6 > x), search left: high=1. Step 2: low=0, high=1, mid=0 (arr[0]=4 < x), search right: low=1. Step 3: low=1, high=1, mid=1 (arr[1]=5 == x). Set result=1, search left: high=0. Step 4: low=1 > high=0, exit loop. Return result=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Modified Binary Search for Boundary Finding
ladders.desc: Adapt binary search to not stop at first match, but continue searching to find boundary indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: modifying standard binary search to find boundaries (first or last occurrence) instead of any occurrence. The intuition: binary search divides the search space, but when we find x, we don't stop; we adjust the search to the left (for first) or right (for last) to check for earlier/later occurrences. This works because the array is sorted, so all x's are grouped together. For first occurrence: when arr[mid] == x, set result = mid and search left (high = mid - 1). For last occurrence: when arr[mid] == x, set result = mid and search right (low = mid + 1). The key difference from standard binary search is the 'result' variable and continued search after finding x. Time: O(log n) as we still halve search space. Space: O(1). This builds into L1 by being the logic used in the helper functions. Common mistakes: not initializing result, incorrect bound updates leading to missed occurrences, confusion between first and last search directions. Real code pattern for first: while low <= high: mid = (low+high)//2; if arr[mid] == x: result = mid; high = mid - 1; elif arr[mid] < x: low = mid + 1; else: high = mid - 1.

examples.input: Find first occurrence in arr = [1, 2, 2, 3], x = 2
examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=3, mid=1 (arr[1]=2 == x). Set result=1, search left: high=0. Step 2: low=0, high=0, mid=0 (arr[0]=1 < x), search right: low=1. Step 3: low=1 > high=0, exit. Result=1.

examples.input: Find last occurrence in arr = [1, 2, 2, 3], x = 2
examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=3, mid=1 (arr[1]=2 == x). Set result=1, search right: low=2. Step 2: low=2, high=3, mid=2 (arr[2]=2 == x). Set result=2, search right: low=3. Step 3: low=3, high=3, mid=3 (arr[3]=3 > x), search left: high=2. Step 4: low=3 > high=2, exit. Result=2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Standard Binary Search on Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Implement binary search to find if an element exists in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is essential because the modified version builds upon standard binary search. The intuition: in a sorted array, we can repeatedly divide the search interval in half, comparing the middle element to the target x. If x equals the middle element, we found it. If x is less, search the left half; if greater, search the right half. This works due to the sorted order, allowing elimination of half the elements each step. Algorithm: 1) Initialize low=0, high=n-1. 2) While low <= high: a) mid = low + (high - low)//2 (avoids overflow). b) If arr[mid] == x: return mid. c) Else if arr[mid] < x: low = mid + 1. d) Else: high = mid - 1. 3) Return -1 if not found. Time: O(log n). Space: O(1). This connects to L2 as the foundation for modifying to find boundaries. Common mistakes: using mid = (low+high)//2 which can overflow, forgetting to update low/high correctly, off-by-one in loop condition. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, x): low, high = 0, len(arr)-1; while low <= high: mid = low + (high - low)//2; if arr[mid] == x: return mid; elif arr[mid] < x: low = mid + 1; else: high = mid - 1; return -1.

examples.trace: Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=2 (arr[2]=5 == x). Return 2.
Step 1: low=0, high=4, mid=2 (arr[2]=6 < 7), low=3. Step 2: low=3, high=4, mid=3 (arr[3]=8 > 7), high=2. Step 3: low=3 > high=2, exit loop. Return -1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Indexing and Comparison
ladders.desc: Access array elements by index and compare values to determine order.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves retrieving an element from an array using its index and comparing it with another value. The intuition: arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has an index starting from 0. By accessing arr[i], we get the value at position i. Comparison (e.g., arr[i] < x) tells us how this value relates to x, which is crucial for binary search decisions. In binary search, we compare the middle element to x to decide which half to search next. This operation is O(1) time and space. It connects to L3 as the core action within binary search loops. Common mistakes: accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[-1] or arr[n]), confusing index with value, incorrect comparison operators. Real code pattern: value = arr[index]; if value == target: ...; elif value < target: ...; else: ... .

examples.trace: Step 1: Access arr[1] gives value 20. Step 2: Compare 20 < 25 is true.
Step 1: Access arr[2] gives value 25. Step 2: Compare 25 == 25 is true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Recognize that in a sorted array, elements are arranged in non-decreasing order.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a sorted array has elements ordered such that each element is less than or equal to the next one (non-decreasing). For example, [1, 3, 3, 5] is sorted because 1 ≤ 3 ≤ 3 ≤ 5. The intuition: this property allows efficient search algorithms like binary search because we can make decisions based on comparisons. If we check a middle element and it's greater than our target, we know the target must be in the left half (if it exists). This ordering is why binary search works. Time/space: not applicable as this is a property, not an algorithm. It connects to L4 by providing the context for why comparisons matter. Common mistakes: confusing sorted with strictly increasing (no duplicates), not checking array boundaries. Real code pattern: checking if array is sorted: for i in range(1, len(arr)): if arr[i] < arr[i-1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compare 2 and 4: 2 ≤ 4, yes. Step 2: Compare 4 and 6: 4 ≤ 6, yes. Step 3: Compare 6 and 8: 6 ≤ 8, yes. All adjacent pairs satisfy non-decreasing order, so sorted.
Step 1: Compare 5 and 3: 5 ≤ 3? No (5 > 3). Found violation, so not sorted.

approaches.name: HashMap O(n)
approaches.summary: Build a frequency map by iterating through the array, storing counts in a hash table, then retrieve the count for x. This is useful if multiple queries for different x are needed.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Occurrences Using HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count how many times a number x appears in an array using a hash map.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that we need to efficiently count occurrences of a specific number in an array. A hash map (dictionary) provides O(1) average time complexity for insertions and lookups, making it ideal for this task. We iterate through the array once, building a frequency map where each key is a number from the array and its value is how many times it appears. After constructing this map, we simply look up the count for the target number x. This approach is particularly useful when we need to answer multiple queries for different numbers, as we build the map once and then answer each query in O(1) time. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Initialize an empty hash map (dictionary). 2. Iterate through each element in the array. 3. For each element, if it's already in the map, increment its count by 1; otherwise, add it to the map with count 1. 4. After processing all elements, check if x exists in the map. If yes, return its count; otherwise, return 0. Time complexity is O(n) for building the map, where n is the array length. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of distinct elements in the array, which in worst case is O(n). Connection to higher levels: This is the complete implementation of the HashMap approach, building upon the key subroutine of building the frequency map. Common mistakes include not handling the case when x is not in the map (should return 0), or using inefficient data structures like lists for lookups. Real code pattern in Python-style pseudocode: def count_occurrences(arr, x): freq = {} for num in arr: if num in freq: freq[num] += 1 else: freq[num] = 1 return freq.get(x, 0).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty freq map: {}. Step 2: Process arr[0]=1: 1 not in map, add: {1:1}. Step 3: Process arr[1]=2: 2 not in map, add: {1:1, 2:1}. Step 4: Process arr[2]=3: 3 not in map, add: {1:1, 2:1, 3:1}. Step 5: Process arr[3]=2: 2 in map, increment: {1:1, 2:2, 3:1}. Step 6: Process arr[4]=1: 1 in map, increment: {1:2, 2:2, 3:1}. Step 7: Process arr[5]=2: 2 in map, increment: {1:2, 2:3, 3:1}. Step 8: Process arr[6]=4: 4 not in map, add: {1:2, 2:3, 3:1, 4:1}. Step 9: Look up x=2 in map: freq[2]=3. Return 3.

Step 1: Initialize empty freq map: {}. Step 2: Process arr[0]=5: 5 not in map, add: {5:1}. Step 3: Process arr[1]=5: 5 in map, increment: {5:2}. Step 4: Process arr[2]=5: 5 in map, increment: {5:3}. Step 5: Process arr[3]=5: 5 in map, increment: {5:4}. Step 6: Look up x=5 in map: freq[5]=4. Return 4.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Build Frequency Map
ladders.desc: Construct a hash map that stores the count of each unique element in an array.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to count occurrences efficiently, we need a data structure that allows quick updates and lookups. A hash map (dictionary) is perfect because it provides average O(1) time for insertions and accesses. We iterate through the array once, and for each element, we either initialize its count to 1 (if it's the first time we see it) or increment its existing count. This builds a complete frequency distribution of all elements. The step-by-step algorithm: 1. Create an empty dictionary (hash map). 2. Loop through each element in the array. 3. For each element, check if it exists as a key in the dictionary. 4. If it exists, increment its value by 1. 5. If it doesn't exist, add it as a key with value 1. 6. After the loop, the dictionary contains all frequencies. Time complexity is O(n) for the single pass through n elements. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the number of distinct elements. Connection to level above: This subroutine is the core of the full solution—once we have the frequency map, counting a specific x is trivial. Common mistakes include using a list instead of a dictionary (which would make lookups O(n)), or forgetting to handle the first occurrence specially. Real code pattern: def build_freq_map(arr): freq = {} for num in arr: if num in freq: freq[num] += 1 else: freq[num] = 1 return freq.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize freq = {}. Step 2: Process 1: not in freq, add {1:1}. Step 3: Process 2: not in freq, add {1:1, 2:1}. Step 4: Process 1: in freq, increment to {1:2, 2:1}. Step 5: Process 3: not in freq, add {1:2, 2:1, 3:1}. Step 6: Process 2: in freq, increment to {1:2, 2:2, 3:1}. Final freq map: {1:2, 2:2, 3:1}.
Step 1: Initialize freq = {}. Step 2: Process 5: not in freq, add {5:1}. Step 3: Process 5: in freq, increment to {5:2}. Step 4: Process 5: in freq, increment to {5:3}. Final freq map: {5:3}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Increment Count in Hash Map
ladders.desc: Update the count of an element in a hash map: increment if exists, initialize to 1 if not.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that when building a frequency map, we need to handle two cases for each array element: if we've seen it before, we increment its count; if it's new, we start counting at 1. This conditional update is the heart of frequency counting. The logic ensures that every element gets counted exactly once per occurrence. The step-by-step algorithm for a single element: 1. Check if the element exists as a key in the hash map. 2. If it exists, access its current value and add 1 to it. 3. If it doesn't exist, add the element as a key with value 1. This operation is O(1) on average for hash maps. Connection to level above: This logic is repeated for each element in the array to build the complete frequency map. Common mistakes include trying to increment a non-existent key (which causes an error) or using inefficient conditional checks. Real code pattern for a single update: if key in map: map[key] += 1 else: map[key] = 1. Time complexity for this single operation is O(1) average. Space complexity may increase by O(1) if adding a new key.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if key=1 exists in map: yes (value is 2). Step 2: Increment value: 2 + 1 = 3. Step 3: Update map: {1:3, 3:1}. Final map: {1:3, 3:1}.
Step 1: Check if key=5 exists in map: no. Step 2: Add key=5 with value 1. Step 3: Map becomes {2:1, 5:1}. Final map: {2:1, 5:1}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Check and Update Dictionary Key
ladders.desc: Determine if a key exists in a dictionary and either retrieve or set its value.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that dictionaries (hash maps) store key-value pairs, and we often need to check for a key's existence before performing operations. This is fundamental to avoiding errors when accessing non-existent keys. The operation involves: first checking if the key is present using the 'in' operator or similar, then either retrieving its current value or setting a new value. This is a common pattern in many algorithms beyond frequency counting. The step-by-step process: 1. Use a conditional to check if the key exists in the dictionary. 2. If it exists, you can access its value (e.g., for reading or modification). 3. If it doesn't exist, you might want to add it with a default value. Time complexity for checking key existence is O(1) on average for hash maps. Space complexity may increase if adding a new key. Connection to level above: This building block is used in the core logic to decide whether to increment an existing count or initialize a new one. Common mistakes include not checking existence before access (leading to KeyError) or using inefficient methods like iterating through all keys. Real code pattern: if key in dict: value = dict[key] else: dict[key] = default_value.

examples.trace: Step 1: Check if key='a' exists in dict: yes. Step 2: Retrieve value: dict['a'] = 10. Step 3: Return 10. (No update needed since key exists).
Step 1: Check if key='y' exists in dict: no. Step 2: Add key='y' with default value 1: dict becomes {'x':5, 'y':1}. Step 3: Return 1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterate Through Array
ladders.desc: Loop through each element in an array to perform an operation.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that to process all elements in a collection, we need to visit each one exactly once. Iteration is a fundamental programming concept used in almost all algorithms that involve arrays or lists. We use a loop (like a for-loop) to go through each element from start to end, allowing us to apply some operation—such as counting, summing, or transforming—to every item. The step-by-step process: 1. Initialize a loop that runs from the first index to the last index of the array. 2. In each iteration, access the current element using its index or directly in a for-each style. 3. Perform the desired operation with that element. 4. Continue until all elements are processed. Time complexity is O(n) for an array of length n, as we touch each element once. Space complexity is O(1) extra space if we don't create new data structures. Connection to level above: This operation is used in the building block to check and update dictionary keys for each array element. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds, modifying the array while iterating, or using inefficient nested loops unnecessarily. Real code pattern: for element in array: # do something with element.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start loop. Step 2: First iteration: element=1, print 1. Step 3: Second iteration: element=2, print 2. Step 4: Third iteration: element=3, print 3. Step 5: Loop ends. Output: 1, 2, 3.

examples.input: arr = [5, 10, 15], operation = add to sum (initial sum=0)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize sum=0. Step 2: Start loop. Step 3: First iteration: element=5, sum becomes 0+5=5. Step 4: Second iteration: element=10, sum becomes 5+10=15. Step 5: Third iteration: element=15, sum becomes 15+15=30. Step 6: Loop ends. Final sum=30.

testCases.input: arr = [5, 5, 5], operation = check if all equal

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Access Array Element
ladders.desc: Retrieve a value from an array using its index.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and each element has a unique index (usually starting from 0). Accessing an element means using its index to get its value. This is the most basic operation for working with arrays and is prerequisite to any iteration or processing. The step-by-step process: 1. Identify the array and the index you want to access. 2. Ensure the index is within the valid range (0 to length-1). 3. Use the index to retrieve the value stored at that position. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] gives 10, arr[1] gives 20, etc. Time complexity is O(1) for direct index access. Space complexity is O(1) as no extra space is needed. Connection to level above: This foundation is used in iteration to access each element sequentially. Common mistakes include using an index out of bounds (which causes an error), confusing zero-based indexing, or misunderstanding that indices are integers. Real code pattern: value = array[index].

examples.trace: Step 1: Array arr has elements [5, 10, 15]. Step 2: Index 1 refers to the second element (since indexing starts at 0). Step 3: Access arr[1]: value is 10. Output: 10.
Step 1: Array arr has elements [100, 200, 300, 400]. Step 2: Index 3 refers to the fourth element. Step 3: Access arr[3]: value is 400. Output: 400.
